mnemonics.title: Memory Hierarchy Order
mnemonics.content: REGISTERS CACHE RAM DISK TAPE - RCRDT (REMEMBER CATS ARE REALLY COOL AND Fun)

acronyms.usage: Used for high-speed data transfer between I/O devices and memory without CPU involvement.
Performs arithmetic and logical operations in the CPU.
The main processor that executes instructions in a computer.
Non-volatile memory containing the bootstrap program.
Volatile main memory that can be read and written.
Hardware component that handles memory protection and virtual memory.
Code that executes when an interrupt is triggered.
Firmware that initializes hardware and loads the operating system.

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted pyramid: Top (smallest, fastest): Registers → Cache → Main Memory → Disk → Tape (largest, slowest). Label each level with typical access time and capacity.
Draw three horizontal parallel lines labeled Data Bus, Address Bus, and Control Bus connecting CPU, Memory, and I/O Devices. Show arrows indicating direction of data flow.
Draw a flowchart: CPU executing → Interrupt occurs → CPU saves state → Fetch ISR → Execute ISR → Restore state → Resume execution. Use boxes and arrows.

analogies.analogy: Think of CPU as a kitchen chef and memory as the pantry. The chef (CPU) needs ingredients (data) from the pantry (memory) to cook (process). Cache is like the kitchen counter where frequently used items are kept for quick access.

The operating system is like a office manager. Hardware is the office infrastructure (building, furniture). Users are employees. The OS coordinates who uses what resources, schedules work, and prevents conflicts.

Imagine DMA as a delivery service that brings packages directly to your doorstep without you (CPU) having to go to the post office each time. You tell the service where to deliver, and it handles the rest while you do other work.
